title: What Am I Gripping About My Sleep?
subtitle: Journal prompts for examining the effort and control around sleep
excerpt: Five journal prompts from Day 22 of a 30-day sleep program. The prompts look at the effort habits around sleep: where the trying is concentrated, what it would mean to stop, and what the body does when it is not trying.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 22 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to examine the effortful habits around sleep — where the trying lives, what it costs, and what the release of it might look like.

content.text: Five journal prompts for examining the effort and control patterns around sleep. The practice moves from mapping what the trying actually looks like, to what it would cost to stop, to what the body does when it is not managing. Set twenty minutes and write without editing.
New to journalling as practice? Read
What does my trying look like at night?
Name the specific habits: counting hours, checking the clock, adjusting the pillow, running through a checklist. What is the actual trying?
When have I slept well without trying — on holiday, in someone else's bed, after a long day outdoors? What was different?
Write what was absent, not just what was present.
What am I gripping about my sleep?
Write the actual fear. Not the polished version — the one that keeps you checking.
If I gave myself permission to stop trying to manage sleep for one night, what would happen?
Write the fear first. Then write what might also be possible.
What would it feel like to hand sleep back to the body and trust it?
Write the physical feeling of that trust. Not the plan — the sensation.
Adapted from Day 22 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
